source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 17 Conclusions - vim & fstab tips

options: :x only writes the file if it was modified, so it doesn't needlessly change the modification time
:x always writes; :wq only writes if changed
They are completely identical in every case
questions.why: :x writes only when there are changes (unlike :wq which always writes), sparing the mtime - which matters to make/file watchers. ZZ in normal mode does the same.

questions.q: In vim ex mode, save and quit using the two-keystroke shortcut.
questions.why: :x saves and quits (writing only if modified); the normal-mode equivalent is ZZ.

questions.q: At the end of an /etc/fstab line there are two integers. What does the FIRST one control?
options: Whether the filesystem is included in backups by the dump utility (0 = no, 1 = yes)
questions.why: The first integer is the dump/backup flag; the second is the fsck check order.

questions.q: What does the SECOND fstab integer control, and what does 1 mean there?
options: The fsck check order at boot: 0 = don't check, 1 = check first (reserved for /), 2 = check afterwards
The dump backup flag; 1 = back up
questions.why: The second integer sets fsck order: 0 no check, 1 first (the root filesystem), 2 the other partitions.
